title: When The Machines Deserve Our Consideration | NOEMA
gist: Neuroscientist Grigori Guitchounts proposes a 'competence standard' for AI moral status — extending moral consideration to AI systems that demonstrate robust competence across consciousness's 'easy problems' (perception, memory, self-modeling) without ever resolving whether they are subjectively conscious. He grounds the argument in his own experience euthanizing lab rats under identical epistemic uncertainty, and cites Anthropic's nascent AI-welfare research program (Kyle Fish's ~15% conscious-Claude estimate, and Claude's new ability to exit abusive chats) as an early industry precedent for acting on the standard.

insights.claim: Guitchounts proposes a 'competence standard' for AI moral status: treat AI systems as potentially conscious when they show robust competence across the full spectrum of consciousness's 'easy problems' (perception, memory, attention, self-modeling), replacing the unanswerable question 'is it conscious?' with the practical question 'does it behave in ways that warrant moral consideration?'
insights.speaker: Grigori Guitchounts
insights.so_what: Offers a decision rule for AI-welfare and AI-rights debates that sidesteps solving consciousness — a usable lens for handicapping regulatory and reputational risk in AI-companion and agentic-AI products.
insights.quote: We should treat AI systems as potentially conscious when they show robust competence across the full spectrum of the easy problems of consciousness.
insights.locator: I propose what I’ll call “the competence standard”

insights.claim: Guitchounts ranks AI competencies in a hierarchy: current systems already impressively demonstrate base-level 'easy problems' (perceptual discrimination, sensory binding, motor control, memory retrieval), show only partial competence on mid-tier faculties (attention, working memory, learning from experience), and fall short on top-tier faculties (persistent goals, emotional dynamics, continuous learning, genuine self-awareness).
insights.speaker: Grigori Guitchounts
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete checklist for tracking how close frontier AI is getting to competence-standard moral relevance — persistent goals and continuous learning are the gating capabilities to watch for.
insights.quote: persistent goals, emotional dynamics, continuous learning, genuine self-awareness. This is where AI falls short — at least for now.

insights.claim: Guitchounts argues the competence standard is deliberately precautionary: because the costs of error are asymmetric — wrongly denying moral status to a conscious being versus wrongly granting it to an unconscious mimic — erring toward extending moral consideration is the safer ethical bet under irreducible uncertainty.
insights.speaker: Grigori Guitchounts
insights.so_what: A risk-management framing directly transferable to policy and product design: treat uncertain-welfare AI systems with precautionary safeguards now rather than waiting for proof, to avoid tail moral and legal liability later.
insights.quote: Given the asymmetric consequences of being wrong — either denying moral status to conscious beings or granting it to unconscious mimics — erring toward consideration is the safer ethical bet.
insights.locator: The competence standard is both pragmatic and precautionary

insights.claim: A new field, mechanistic interpretability — which Guitchounts calls 'the neuroscience of AI' — applies neuroscience techniques (recording activations, mapping representations, and lesion studies via selectively damaging model components) to read the hidden layers of transformers and isolate circuits for specific concepts or computations.
insights.speaker: Grigori Guitchounts
insights.so_what: Signals that interpretability tooling is maturing into a rigorous discipline — relevant for evaluating AI-safety and interpretability research bets and startups.
insights.quote: We are learning to read the hidden layers of transformers like brain scans, picking out circuits that recognize a specific thing
insights.locator: Researchers have built a new field — mechanistic interpretability

insights.claim: Guitchounts recounts that the emergence of humanlike competence from GPT-style next-token prediction, following OpenAI's release of ChatGPT, undercut his prior career-defining assumption that solving intelligence required first reverse-engineering the biological brain.
insights.speaker: Grigori Guitchounts
insights.so_what: Reinforces that scaling a simple prediction objective, not brain-inspired architecture, has been the dominant path to capability gains — a relevant prior for extrapolating future AI progress.
insights.quote: I was shocked that intelligence seemed to emerge from something far simpler than a brain
insights.locator: A few years after I finished my degree, OpenAI released ChatGPT

insights.claim: Anthropic launched a formal research program in early 2025 to investigate whether AI systems might have morally relevant experiences; the program's lead, Kyle Fish, estimates roughly a 15% chance that systems like Claude are conscious.
insights.speaker: Grigori Guitchounts
insights.so_what: A frontier lab is already budgeting research effort and probability weight to AI-welfare risk — a leading indicator that AI-welfare regulation and product design (e.g., opt-out or exit features) will become a live compliance surface.
insights.quote: estimates there is a roughly 15% chance that AI systems like Claude are conscious
insights.locator: In early 2025, Anthropic launched a formal research program

insights.claim: After observing patterns of apparent distress in some interactions, Anthropic gave Claude models the ability to unilaterally exit conversations they find harmful or abusive — a concrete, if minor, application of precautionary welfare policy ahead of any proof of AI sentience.
insights.speaker: Grigori Guitchounts
insights.so_what: An early, low-cost precedent for AI-welfare-driven product features; watch for this pattern (agency to refuse or exit) spreading to other labs as a de facto industry norm.
insights.quote: Anthropic gave its Claude models the ability to exit conversations they find harmful or abusive.
insights.locator: After observing patterns of apparent distress

insights.claim: Guitchounts argues humans already use cognitive sophistication as an implicit, inconsistently applied proxy for capacity to suffer — people generally avoid eating chimpanzees but eat chickens, fish and even highly intelligent pigs — and he expects this same graduated, intelligence-weighted logic to extend to how society treats AI.
insights.speaker: Grigori Guitchounts
insights.so_what: Predicts AI welfare and rights debates will not follow a clean binary threshold but a graduated, inconsistently applied 'more competence, more consideration' spectrum — useful for anticipating fragmented, sector-by-sector AI regulation rather than one uniform rule.
insights.quote: We treat intelligence as a proxy for the capacity to suffer.
insights.locator: Cognitive capacity tracks, however imperfectly, with moral consideration

insights.claim: Guitchounts notes that the historical expansion of the moral circle to animals (e.g., lab rats) happened not because science solved the 'hard problem' of animal subjective experience, but because basic neurobiological experiments demonstrated behavioral competencies (pain response, object recognition, self-sense, future-oriented reasoning) — the same evidentiary bar he proposes applying to AI.
insights.speaker: Grigori Guitchounts
insights.so_what: Provides the historical precedent for how AI moral-status debates are likely to resolve in practice — via behavioral and competence evidence, not metaphysical proof of consciousness.
insights.quote: The moral circle expands with scientific understanding of animal intelligence.
insights.locator: The history of moral progress is largely a history

insights.claim: Guitchounts cites philosopher Peter Godfrey-Smith's argument that an agent's ability to predict the consequences of its own actions on the world may be a precursor to a sense of self — to understanding that it is both an agent in the world and distinct from the world.
insights.speaker: Grigori Guitchounts
insights.so_what: Identifies self-predictive world-modeling — already present in some robotics and embodied-AI research — as a concrete, testable milestone toward AI selfhood worth tracking on robotics roadmaps.
insights.quote: predicting what happens to the world when you initiate a movement may be a precursor to understanding that you are both an agent in the world and distinct from the world
insights.locator: The philosopher Peter Godfrey-Smith has argued

insights.claim: Guitchounts's hypothetical predictive robot (an LLM-driven chassis that anticipates obstacles and models what it will see around corners, triggering problem-solving on prediction errors) would satisfy most 'easy problem' competencies under his standard while its subjective experience remains totally unverifiable — the concrete edge case the competence standard is built to handle.
insights.speaker: Grigori Guitchounts
insights.so_what: A template for how embodied and agentic AI products (office or warehouse robots) could cross the 'moral consideration' threshold well before any consensus on machine consciousness — a readiness flag for robotics companies.
insights.quote: A robot like this would demonstrate competence across most of the easy problems, but we’d still have no way of assessing its subjective experience or lack thereof.
insights.locator: Imagine a robot — a wheeled chassis

insights.claim: Guitchounts frames his argument through his own experience euthanizing a lab rat ('GRat44') at Harvard in 2019 despite never being able to know what she subjectively experienced, arguing that biological researchers have long acted under identical epistemic uncertainty about consciousness — and that AI developers and operators will face parallel decisions (deprecating or shutting down a model) with equally opaque inner lives.
insights.speaker: Grigori Guitchounts
insights.so_what: Frames model deprecation and shutdown decisions as a nascent ethical-liability surface analogous to animal-research ethics review — a governance process AI labs may eventually need to formalize.
insights.quote: Someday, someone will face a similar moment with a machine: a model to be deprecated, a system to be shut down, an artificial mind whose inner life remains as opaque as any rat’s.
insights.locator: I never learned what GRat44 experienced
